Client 2: And I’m Linda. Same—also I want to feel like we have enough cushion, like liquid-wise, even if the market’s weird.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill down. Roughly, what’s the household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: Combined, it’s 172200 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in—mortgage or rent, bills, lifestyle, the whole thing?
Client 2: We track it loosely, but it’s 9400 a month.

Client 1: Combined, it’s 172200 annually.

Client 2: We track it loosely, but it’s 9400 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And do you have any monthly debt payments—like car loans, student loans, credit cards you’re paying down—anything like that?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client 2: And I’m Linda. Same—also I want to feel like we have enough cushion, like liquid-wise, even if the market’s weird.

Advisor: Great—hang on, I’m just noting that. Now, shifting to what you’ve built up: about how much do you consider investable assets—retirement accounts, brokerage, anything you’d invest—total?
Client 2: I always think of it as like mid-six hundreds, but the total investable assets is 647100.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s unpack what’s inside that so I understand what’s where. David, what retirement accounts are in your name?
Client 1: I’ve got a workplace retirement account—my 401k-style one—at 203550.

Client 2: Yes, there’s a private markets investment on an advisor platform—48,750.
Advisor: Thanks. And then housing—do you own your home together?
Client 1: Yes, it’s joint—our primary residence is valued at 350,050.
Advisor: Helpful. Just to make sure I’m not missing anything big: you said no loans outstanding and no monthly debt payments—so no mortgage, no car note, nothing like that?
Client 2: Right, nothing. No mortgage—no payments.

Advisor: Perfect. Let’s unpack what’s inside that so I understand what’s where. David, what retirement accounts are in your name?
Client 1: I’ve got a workplace retirement account—my 401k-style one—at 203550.
Advisor: Okay, and any other accounts on your side—like an IRA or an old plan sitting somewhere?
Client 1: There’s another one that’s more like a bank-linked retirement account—91,600. Sorry—91,600 exactly.
Advisor: Yep, got it. Linda, what’s on your side in taxable or non-retirement accounts?

Client 2: It’s… honestly it’s not one of the standard ones you probably see a lot. Put it as Other.
Advisor: Okay, I’ll mark state of residence as Other. Thanks.
Advisor: Risk-wise—when markets drop, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, aggressive… where do you land?
Client 1: Conservative. We’re conservative—like, we’d rather give up upside than take a big hit.
Advisor: Makes sense. And just so I’m not mixing terms—when you say conservative, you mean you want smaller swings and more downside protection, right?

Client 2: Yes, exactly—smaller swings. I can’t sleep when it’s bouncing around a lot.
Advisor: Okay. Last household item: your tax bracket band—do you know roughly where you land federally?
Client 1: Yes—37%.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just noting that down.
Advisor: While I’ve got you: we already covered the accounts and the house value, and you both said no loans outstanding and nothing outside work benefits for insurance—still all true as of today?

Client 2: Yeah, nothing outside work benefits.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, David, thanks Linda. Before we wrap this section, I want to capture a couple household basics that drive the plan.
Advisor: First one: are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. Next—state of residence. What state do you file from?

Client 1: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. And then, David, Linda—how are you engaging with an advisor, if at all? Like brokerage, bank, or an independent advisory firm?
Client 1: It’s an RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. And just to keep my notes consistent: when you say RIA, you mean you’re working with a registered investment advisor versus a commission brokerage setup, right?
Client 2: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor), not a commission brokerage thing.

Client 2: And I’m Linda. Same—also I want to feel like we have enough cushion, like liquid-wise, even if the market’s weird.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill down. Roughly, what’s the household gross income per year right now—before taxes?
Client 1: Combined, it’s 172200 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in—mortgage or rent, bills, lifestyle, the whole thing?
Client 2: We track it loosely, but it’s 9400 a month.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Linda—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what would make this meeting feel useful today?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. I’d like a clear plan—retirement timing, what we should do with the accounts, and just making sure we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. Same—also I want to feel like we have enough cushion, like liquid-wise, even if the market’s weird.

Client 1: Yes—employed.
Advisor: Okay. And what kind of work bucket would you put your role in—like finance, tech, sales, operations, that sort of thing?
